metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `tracker_head_sha`'s dirty/unmerged-state rejection is new logic to be written (not a reuse of `signing.head_sha` or `snapshot_head`), and specify exactly which git commands detect those states and what exception/error the function raises when they are present.
evidence: `signing.head_sha` (signing.py:524-541): runs `git -C repo_root rev-parse HEAD`, returns 'unknown' on failure — no dirty/unmerged check, no rejection, swallows all errors.
`snapshot_head` (_concurrency.py:41-60): runs `git rev-parse` on tickets branch via `git_adapter.rev_parse`, no dirty/unmerged check.
No function matching `tracker_head_sha` found anywhere in the codebase (search confirmed zero matches).
No function combining `git rev-parse HEAD` against a tracker WITH dirty/unmerged rejection found anywhere in the codebase.
Dirty/unmerged state detection would require additional git commands (e.g., `git status --porcelain`, `git ls-files -u`) that are absent from any existing plan-review or signing helper.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `tracker_head_sha(tracker)` 'reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker … and rejects dirty or unmerged state.' The only existing git-HEAD helper, `signing.head_sha(repo_root)`, runs `git -C <repo_root> rev-parse HEAD` but performs NO dirty or unmerged-state check anywhere. No other helper in the codebase combines both behaviors. The dirty/unmerged rejection is entirely new implementation work, not a reuse of anything existing — the plan's 'reuses' framing is an unbacked codebase assertion.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker after ensure_fresh and rejects dirty or unmerged state.'
scenarios: An implementer reading 'reuses the repository git helper' may assume the dirty/unmerged rejection comes for free and omit it, producing a `tracker_head_sha` that silently accepts a dirty tracker — violating AC 'tracker_head_sha captures the clean tickets-tracker 40-hex HEAD after ensure_fresh'.
The plan delegates to child 5967 the retry/mismatch behavior that depends on `tracker_head_sha` working correctly, so an incorrect implementation here silently propagates.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reuses the repository git helper' with 'calls `git -C <tracker> rev-parse HEAD` (following the pattern of `signing.head_sha`) and additionally runs `git -C <tracker> status --porcelain` and `git -C <tracker> ls-files -u`, raising a `PlanRelationSnapshotError` with reason `store-read-failure` on any dirty or unmerged output.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the PlanRelationSnapshot spec exactly which ticket states are 'preloaded' for the inbound-blocks scan: if only the subject + named-target states are preloaded, specify a supplementary store-wide scan (mirroring _inbound.py's _mentioning_dirs) to discover arbitrary inbound blockers; if a full store scan is intended, name it explicitly so implementers understand the O(store) cost and can cache accordingly.
evidence: Plan: 'directly scanning relation entries in every preloaded state, including archived sources; do not call find_inbound_relationships'

src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py:39-72 — find_inbound_relationships does a byte-level store-wide scan (_mentioning_dirs) followed by per-candidate reduce_ticket; there is no 'preloaded state set' concept in the current machinery
ABSENCE: The plan never defines what 'every preloaded state' means — is it all tickets in the store, or just the subject + identified targets?
findings.finding: The plan says PlanRelationSnapshot is 'built from one compiled ticket-state preload per review' and inbound blocks are found by 'directly scanning relation entries in every preloaded state' — but does not specify that this preload must cover ALL tickets in the store (not just the subject and named targets). The existing find_inbound_relationships does a byte-level O(store) scan precisely to discover arbitrary tickets that carry a 'blocks' dep pointing at the subject. If the preload covers only the subject + explicit targets, inbound blocks from arbitrary other tickets are silently missed, producing an incomplete prerequisite set.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Build the snapshot from one compiled ticket-state preload per review. Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by directly scanning relation entries in every preloaded state'
scenarios: Ticket B has a 'blocks' dep pointing at subject A, but B is not named in the subject's own deps. If the preload only covers A and A's explicit targets, B is never scanned, and A's inbound-block from B is silently missing from the prerequisite pin set.
The prerequisite pin set is incomplete → the signed manifest does not bind the full prerequisite surface → child 7c27's prerequisite criterion receives a truncated input.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that all tracker_head_sha infrastructure failures (subprocess errors, missing git binary, invalid path) are caught and re-raised as PlanRelationSnapshotError with reason='store-read-failure', ensuring the traceback-free structured error guarantee holds unconditionally.
evidence: The six named PlanRelationSnapshotError reasons include 'store-read-failure' but the plan does not state that tracker_head_sha git-infrastructure errors map to this reason.
The guarantee 'error never produces…traceback fallback' is unconditional, but the error mapping for tracker_head_sha failures is unspecified.
If tracker_head_sha raises an unhandled exception, the review boundary's PlanRelationSnapshotError catch block would miss it.
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() is specified to 'reuse the repository git helper' and 'rejects dirty or unmerged state,' but the plan does not specify how unexpected git infrastructure failures (e.g., git not installed, tracker path invalid, subprocess error) are handled — these could bypass PlanRelationSnapshotError and produce unhandled exceptions, contradicting the 'error never produces a traceback fallback' guarantee.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) reuses the repository git helper…rejects dirty or unmerged state.' / Paragraph 4: 'the error never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback.'
scenarios: git binary is absent in a CI environment — tracker_head_sha raises FileNotFoundError, bypasses structured error path, produces a traceback in the review output.
Tracker repository path is misconfigured — subprocess raises CalledProcessError, same bypass.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add: 'tracker_head_sha wraps all subprocess and IO errors in PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason="store-read-failure", reference=tracker_path) so the review boundary's existing catch block handles them uniformly.' Add a corresponding AC: '[ ] tracker_head_sha subprocess failures raise PlanRelationSnapshotError(store-read-failure) rather than propagating raw exceptions.' Add a test case in test_plan_relation_snapshot.py for this scenario.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which PlanRelationSnapshotError reason (e.g., store-read-failure) tracker_head_sha raises on dirty/unmerged state, and add a test asserting that condition produces the exact structured ERROR record without a traceback.
evidence: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker after ensure_fresh and rejects dirty or unmerged state'
'the error never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback'
Verification names 'tracker HEAD capture' but does NOT name a dirty/unmerged rejection test
Named PlanRelationSnapshotError reasons: missing-target, ambiguous-reference, malformed-reference, reducer-error, canonical-id-mismatch, store-read-failure — dirty/unmerged is absent from this list
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() is specified to reject dirty or unmerged tracker state, but the plan never names what exception it raises in those cases, never maps those states to a PlanRelationSnapshotError reason, and the test plan only asserts 'tracker HEAD capture' (the happy path). If dirty/unmerged raises something other than PlanRelationSnapshotError, the review boundary's catch — which the plan says must never produce a traceback fallback — will silently miss it.
findings.location: Implementation §5 ('tracker_head_sha... rejects dirty or unmerged state') and Verification §2 ('tracker HEAD capture')
scenarios: Reviewer runs plan-review on a repo with a dirty (uncommitted) tickets tracker — tracker_head_sha raises an uncaught non-PlanRelationSnapshotError, bypassing the structured-error boundary and producing a traceback instead of the required INDETERMINATE record.
CI runs against a tickets repo that has an unmerged conflict marker — same escape path.
findings.suggested_fix: Map dirty/unmerged state to store-read-failure (or add a new reason such as dirty-tracker-state) in PlanRelationSnapshotError, document this in the Implementation section, and add a test variant to test_plan_relation_snapshot.py covering 'tracker is dirty → store-read-failure ERROR record, no traceback'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `refresh_attestation` (progressive drift refresh path) acquires a `PlanRelationSnapshot` — either by adding a `ticket_id`-based snapshot build using the existing tracker read, or by passing the snapshot from the orchestrator call-site — so the plan's 'no new attestation minted pinless' invariant is implementable for this path.
evidence: Plan: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, progressive drift refresh, and resign_plan_review—collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes related_material; no new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless.'
refresh_attestation signature in attest.py: def refresh_attestation(ticket_id: str, prior_manifest: list[str], *, probe: str, repo_root=None) — no verdict dict, no subject state, no children list
refresh_attestation builds manifest via: manifest = build_manifest(fields, material=..., deps=new_deps, ...) — no pins argument possible without a snapshot source
The prior manifest does not carry child or prerequisite state needed to build a fresh PlanRelationSnapshot
findings.finding: refresh_attestation (attest.py lines 350-385) is one of the three named signing paths that must emit a complete pin set, but unlike sign_plan_review or resign_plan_review it reconstructs a manifest from the PRIOR manifest (not a fresh PlanContext/verdict) and accepts no ticket-state input. The plan says 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes related_material', yet it never specifies how refresh_attestation — whose signature is (ticket_id, prior_manifest, *, probe, repo_root) with no depends_on/blocks scan context — acquires the PlanRelationSnapshot.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Extend build_manifest(…, *, pins …)' / attest.py refresh_attestation (lines 350-385)
scenarios: A drift-refresh re-sign on a ticket whose children changed since the original review would emit pins from the prior (now stale) relation snapshot, or would need an extra ticket-store read not described by the plan.
An implementer following the plan literally cannot determine what ticket-store reads refresh_attestation should perform to build the PlanRelationSnapshot, since the plan omits this entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `tracker_head_sha()` raises `PlanRelationSnapshotError` for dirty/unmerged state (and if so, which reason value from the closed enum — e.g. `store-read-failure`), or define a separate exception type that the review-boundary catch also handles, so the pre-LLM abort and no-traceback-fallback guarantees are enforceable for this failure mode.
evidence: Plan: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker after ensure_fresh and rejects dirty or unmerged state.'

Plan: 'The review boundary catches it [PlanRelationSnapshotError] before any LLM call, emits an ERROR record exactly {event: plan_relation_snapshot_error, reason: <enum>, canonical_id: string|null, reference: string|null}, and returns unsigned INDETERMINATE remediation through CLI and MCP; the error never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback.'

ABSENCE: the plan never states whether dirty/unmerged state raises PlanRelationSnapshotError (and if so, with which of the six named reason values), a different exception type, or some other signal. The six named reasons all describe target-resolution failures, not tracker-state failures.

If dirty/unmerged raises a plain exception (e.g. ValueError/RuntimeError), the boundary catch on PlanRelationSnapshotError misses it entirely, breaking the 'never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback' and 'before any LLM call' guarantees.
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha()'s 'dirty or unmerged state' rejection protocol is named but its exception type is never specified, leaving the review-boundary catch (which is keyed on PlanRelationSnapshotError) unable to guarantee pre-LLM termination and no-traceback-fallback for this failure mode.
findings.location: Implementation § tracker_head_sha / § review boundary catch / AC: 'Every unreadable or unresolvable target returns unsigned INDETERMINATE before any LLM call'
scenarios: Executor maps dirty-tracker rejection to a plain ValueError; the review-boundary catch only catches PlanRelationSnapshotError; the ValueError propagates as a traceback, violating the structured-error contract.

Executor adds a seventh 'tracker-dirty' reason to PlanRelationSnapshotError; tests written for six reasons silently miss this path; AC 'Every named PlanRelationSnapshotError reason produces the exact structured ERROR record' is under-specified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'A dirty or unmerged tracker raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason="store-read-failure") with no target reference, so the review boundary's existing PlanRelationSnapshotError catch covers it.' This closes the enum/catch gap without adding a new reason value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the behavior of PlanRelationSnapshot collection in resign_plan_review and progressive drift refresh when a prerequisite becomes unresolvable post-review: either (a) define that these re-sign paths use best-effort pin collection (recording available pins and warning on unresolvable targets rather than aborting), or (b) explicitly accept that re-signing a previously-valid PASS is blocked by a subsequently-deleted prerequisite and document this as intentional.
evidence: Plan: 'Every unreadable or unresolvable target returns unsigned INDETERMINATE before any LLM call'
Plan: 'resign_plan_review emits the complete current pin set'

src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py:35-148 — resign_plan_review is a NO-LLM path: it reads a sidecar, validates the material fingerprint, reconstructs the verdict, then calls attest.sign_plan_review()
ABSENCE: The plan gives no escape hatch or differentiated error handling for the resign path when a prerequisite becomes unresolvable post-review
findings.finding: The resign_plan_review path (no-LLM sidecar recovery) calls sign_plan_review, which the plan requires to collect a PlanRelationSnapshot and embed pins. But resign_plan_review reads a sidecar (not a live review), and the plan's abort rule states that any unreadable/unresolvable target raises PlanRelationSnapshotError → unsigned INDETERMINATE before any LLM call. This means a ticket whose prerequisite was deleted after the original PASS review was computed can never be re-signed, even though the sidecar verdict is still valid and the plan itself is unchanged. The plan does not acknowledge this behavioral change to resign_plan_review.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, progressive drift refresh, and resign_plan_review—collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes related_material'
scenarios: A ticket passes review with prerequisite P. P is subsequently deleted. The agent needs to re-sign (e.g. after a signing failure). resign_plan_review collects a PlanRelationSnapshot, finds P unresolvable, raises PlanRelationSnapshotError, and returns unsigned INDETERMINATE — blocking recovery even though the underlying verdict and material fingerprint are still valid.
Progressive drift refresh (which also calls build_manifest with pins via refresh_attestation) has the same issue: if a prerequisite is deleted after the review, the drift refresh cannot re-sign.

evidence: 26 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and a corresponding test assertion) verifying that ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins are importable from attest.py.
evidence: 'Define ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins() in manifest.py; attest.py imports and re-exports both beside the existing manifest helpers.'
ABSENCE: No AC reads 'attest.py exports ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins' or equivalent.
findings.finding: The described deliverable 'attest.py imports and re-exports both [ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins] beside the existing manifest helpers' has no covering acceptance criterion. No AC verifies that these symbols are importable from attest.py.
findings.location: Implementation — 'attest.py imports and re-exports both beside the existing manifest helpers'; Acceptance Criteria (absence)
scenarios: If the re-export is omitted (e.g. a developer only adds them to manifest.py), all ACs pass but consumer code importing from attest.py breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins are importable from attest.py alongside existing manifest helpers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether tracker_head_sha() can be implemented as a thin wrapper over the existing `snapshot_head()` in `_concurrency.py` (adding dirty/unmerged validation on top) rather than re-implementing the rev-parse step, to avoid duplicating already-tested git-HEAD logic.
evidence: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_concurrency.py lines 41-63: snapshot_head(repo_root: Path) -> str runs git rev-parse against the tickets branch/HEAD and returns the 40-hex SHA
20+ call-sites for snapshot_head across the reconciler test suite and production code
Plan describes tracker_head_sha as defined in a new plan_review/relation_snapshot.py module that 'imports no signing or orchestration modules'
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() rebuilds snapshot_head() functionality already present in the codebase. The plan defines a new function in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py that 'reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker after ensure_fresh,' which is exactly what snapshot_head() in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_concurrency.py (lines 41-63) does — it runs git rev-parse against the tracker ref and returns the 40-hex HEAD SHA, with fallback handling. snapshot_head() has 20+ call-sites across the reconciler. The new function adds dirty/unmerged rejection, but that incremental guard could be layered on top of the existing helper rather than defining a new function that re-implements the core rev-parse step.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker after ensure_fresh and rejects dirty or unmerged state. Collection stores that 40-hex value as ticket_store_revision.'
scenarios: Implementer writes a fresh rev-parse wrapper duplicating snapshot_head's retry/fallback logic, creating two codepaths to maintain.
Edge-case handling (empty repo, missing ref) diverges from snapshot_head's tested EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL contract.
findings.suggested_fix: In relation_snapshot.py: from rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler._concurrency import snapshot_head; def tracker_head_sha(tracker): ensure_fresh(tracker); sha = snapshot_head(Path(tracker)); <check dirty/unmerged>; return sha — reuses the tested rev-parse path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Merge 'Enabled/Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership' into a single AC (or add a concrete behavioral distinction — e.g. one covers a validation gate that fires only when enabled) to eliminate the near-duplicate.
evidence: 'Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership.'
'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership.'
Implementation: 'Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled.'
findings.finding: Two ACs test identical logical property from symmetric angles without added coverage: 'Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership' and 'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership' both verify that pin membership is invariant to the enforcement flag. Neither tests a distinct scenario the other misses, making them near-duplicates.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership' / 'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership'
scenarios: A reviewer reading the AC set sees two criteria that verify the same invariant and cannot distinguish what would cause one to fail but not the other.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace both with: 'Pin membership is identical regardless of enforcement flag; enforcement controls only downstream validation, not collection.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly justify the 'no pinless attestation' irreversibility as an intentional, permanent constraint OR add a stated rollback/bypass mechanism (e.g. an operator-controlled emergency bypass, or a note that child 5967 covers the remediation path for collection failures).
evidence: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, progressive drift refresh, and resign_plan_review—collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes related_material; no new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless.'
'Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership.' / 'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership.' — confirms enforcement flag is not an escape hatch.
ABSENCE: No rollback path, temporary bypass, or operational runbook reference for systematic pin-collection failure.
findings.finding: The plan mandates 'no new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless' with an enforcement flag that explicitly does not change pin membership, yet provides no rollback or escape-hatch path for scenarios where pin collection is systematically unavailable (e.g., tickets-tracker infrastructure failure, PlanRelationSnapshotError on every review). In that scenario all plan reviews are blocked with no stated remediation.
findings.location: Implementation — 'no new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless'; 'Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled'
scenarios: Tickets-tracker becomes temporarily unavailable; every plan review returns INDETERMINATE with no path forward until infrastructure is restored.
A newly discovered bug in relation_snapshot.py causes PlanRelationSnapshotError on all reviews; there is no feature flag to revert to pinless signing while the bug is fixed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the PlanRelationSnapshot-as-boundary architectural decision in an ADR or AGENTS.md entry so future implementers of child 7c27 and maintainers can discover the contract without reading implementation code.
evidence: No ADR or design-doc update is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
The boundary between this story and 7c27 is a non-obvious architectural contract that future authors must discover by reading implementation code or this ticket.
findings.finding: The architectural decision to expose PlanRelationSnapshot as a typed boundary for future child 7c27 (prerequisite criterion) is described only inline in the Implementation section and is not captured in any ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update, making the contract invisible to future maintainers.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 2: 'Child 7c27 owns registration…this story exposes PlanRelationSnapshot as its typed input boundary but does not implement or assume that criterion exists.'
scenarios: A developer implementing 7c27 changes PlanRelationSnapshot's shape without realizing it's a published boundary, silently breaking the contract.
A maintainer auditing cross-component contracts cannot find the decision rationale in any document.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (or AGENTS.md section) stating: 'PlanRelationSnapshot is a stable typed input boundary for the future prerequisite criterion (7c27); its public fields (subject_state, ticket_states_by_id, child_ids, prerequisite_ids, related_material, ticket_store_revision) form a versioned contract — field removals or renames require coordination with 7c27.' Reference this ADR from the Implementation section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconsider whether PlanRelationSnapshot needs to be a named, separately-housed abstraction now, or whether the pin-collection logic can live inline in sign_plan_review and a typed boundary introduced when child 7c27 is actually scoped (YAGNI — the second consumer does not exist yet).
evidence: Zero existing call-sites for PlanRelationSnapshot (new file plan_review/relation_snapshot.py does not yet exist in codebase)
Plan explicitly states child 7c27 is a *future* story that this story does not implement
Rule-of-Three: only one confirmed consumer (pin collection in sign_plan_review); the second is a hypothetical future child story
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshot is a new named abstraction with exactly one current consumer (the sign_plan_review pin-collection path) and one explicitly hypothetical future consumer (child 7c27, which 'this story does not implement or assume exists'). With only one real call-site at definition time, it fails the Rule-of-Three and YAGNI: the abstraction is justified by a future story that may never land or may need a different interface.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot... in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py... Child 7c27 owns registration, schemas, prompts, and routing for the future prerequisite criterion; this story exposes PlanRelationSnapshot as its typed input boundary but does not implement or assume that criterion exists.'
scenarios: Child 7c27 lands with a different interface need, requiring PlanRelationSnapshot to be redesigned anyway — the abstraction boundary bought nothing.
Child 7c27 is never built, leaving PlanRelationSnapshot as dead abstraction with one caller.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Verification section that test_plan_material_pins.py, test_plan_relation_snapshot.py, and test_plan_review_resign.py are new files to be created (not existing files to be extended), so implementers know they start from scratch rather than searching for pre-existing anchors.
evidence: Search for 'test_plan_review_resign' returns no matches in the repo
Search for 'test_plan_material_pins' returns no matches in the repo
resign.py exists but has no matching test file named test_plan_review_resign.py — the resign path is currently covered by tests inside test_plan_review.py or not at all
The plan does not explicitly flag these as new files to be created
findings.finding: The Verification section names test_plan_review_resign.py as an existing test file proving 'ordinary sign, progressive drift refresh, and re-sign emit complete pins', but this file does not exist in the repo. All three of the named Verification test files (test_plan_material_pins.py, test_plan_relation_snapshot.py, test_plan_review_resign.py) are absent. This is expected for newly-created test files, but the current test_plan_review.py and test_attestation_signing.py are the closest existing counterparts and are not named as files to be extended — creating a potential coverage gap if the implementer doesn't recognise that test_plan_review_resign.py is a wholly new creation.
findings.location: Verification section / tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py
scenarios: An implementer treating Verification test paths as pre-existing anchors would attempt to run 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_material_pins.py …' before implementation and see collection errors rather than test failures, causing confusion about test strategy.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line note to the Verification section: 'All three test modules are new files created as part of this story.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for encoding pins as manifest-text lines rather than as top-level DSSE predicate fields, specifically addressing how the signed_manifest binding in op-cert records provides equivalent authentication and why the expand-contract manifest-line pattern is preferred for this additive field.
evidence: Plan: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — no rationale given for manifest-text vs DSSE-payload encoding

src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:410-428 — _authoritative_material() explicitly sources material_fingerprint from the SIGNED DSSE payload for op-cert records, NOT the manifest text, as a security measure against tampered plaintext
ABSENCE: No 'Rationale' or 'Why this approach' section in the plan
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for encoding pins as manifest-text lines (the same pattern as dep/regver lines) rather than as fields in the DSSE in-toto predicate payload, which is the pattern already used for material_fingerprint in op-cert records. Since pins are security-adjacent (they bind the versions of prerequisites/children the review was certified against), the choice of authentication boundary matters: manifest text in op-cert records is authenticated via signed_manifest in the predicate, while a top-level predicate field would have a cleaner authenticated path and align with how material_fingerprint is stored and read via _authoritative_material.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' / 'Define ManifestFormatError and manifest_pins() in manifest.py'
scenarios: A reviewer reading the plan cannot determine whether the manifest-text approach was chosen deliberately (because signed_manifest in the DSSE predicate authenticates it identically) or by oversight (missing the existing precedent of putting security-relevant fields directly in the predicate).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'Pins are encoded as manifest-text lines (matching the dep/regver/disabled_builtins pattern) rather than as DSSE predicate fields because: (1) the signed_manifest step in the in-toto predicate already authenticates the full manifest for op-cert records (mirroring how dep hashes and regver are authenticated), (2) the expand-contract manifest-line pattern preserves byte-identical legacy manifest parsing, and (3) no new predicate schema change is needed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit prior-art justification in the Implementation section explaining why the custom `plan-material-pin:` plaintext manifest line was chosen over encoding the same information as in-toto-style `materials` fields inside the existing DSSE predicate (referencing ADR 0044's in-toto adoption and the project's existing `src/rebar/attest/opcert.py` Statement/predicate shape).
evidence: Plan quote: 'plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>' — a custom plaintext manifest line format
ADR 0044 (docs/adr/0044-asymmetric-attestation-substrate.md): 'DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard; the encoder is ~30 lines, offline, scheme-agnostic.'
ADR 0049 prior-art section: 'in-toto / SLSA — subject/state binding; non-falsifiable provenance produced by a trusted control plane'
in-toto link attestations define a 'materials' map of {artifact-name: {sha256: hex}} — the exact 'which inputs did this step consume' semantic the pin lines encode
ABSENCE: the plan contains no mention of in-toto link materials, SLSA provenance byProducts/materials, or SBOM (CycloneDX/SPDX) as considered alternatives
The project's src/rebar/attest/ package already ships a full DSSE envelope + in-toto-aligned Statement/predicate substrate (opcert.py, dsse.py)
findings.finding: The plan introduces a custom plaintext `plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>` manifest-line format to record which related-ticket material a plan review relied on — precisely the 'materials used' provenance that in-toto link attestations already model. The project's own ADR 0044 adopted DSSE/in-toto as its signing substrate ('DSSE is the Sigstore / in-toto / SLSA standard') and ADR 0049 cites 'in-toto / SLSA — subject/state binding; non-falsifiable provenance' as prior art for op-certs. The plan neither acknowledges this prior art for the pin-encoding choice nor justifies why pins are bespoke plaintext lines rather than in-toto-style structured `materials` fields embedded in the existing DSSE payload or predicate.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Extend the deterministic manifest so pin lines appear … plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>'
scenarios: A future integrator attempting to verify plan-material pins with an external SLSA or in-toto verifier finds the pins are opaque text tokens in a plan-review manifest, requiring a bespoke parser rather than standard tooling.

The ADR 0044-mandated DSSE signature authenticates the outer envelope but has no structural awareness of pin semantics; pin tampering within a valid envelope relies on a separate manifest-text parser rather than the signed payload structure.

A reviewer or future maintainer familiar with in-toto asks why 'materials' fields inside the existing predicate were not used, finds no justification recorded, and has to reverse-engineer the design intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state explicitly: state whether a pre-feature reader accepts pin-bearing manifests as CERTIFIED (and if not, document the operator recovery path — e.g., `resign_plan_review` strips pins and re-signs).
evidence: The plan adds `plan-material-pin:` lines inside the DSSE-signed manifest payload. Rolled-back code will encounter these extra lines in the manifest list.
The plan specifies a fixture for 'pre-feature reader' behavior but this is tested under the NEW codebase, not under a rolled-back binary.

Absence: the plan contains no statement about post-rollback schema state — e.g., 'a pre-feature verifier will still compute a valid HMAC/DSSE signature over the full manifest including pin lines and treat the attestation as CERTIFIED' or 'operators must re-sign after rollback'.
T11 criterion spec: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0 — a rollback can exit 0 while leaving the schema in an incorrect intermediate state.'
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the persisted-format state AFTER a code rollback. If feature code is rolled back after pin-bearing manifests are written to the ticket event log, the plan does not specify whether a pre-feature reader would accept (CERTIFIED) or reject those manifests. The only rollback evidence is a pre-feature fixture that tests the new reader handling pin lines — not a rolled-back old reader handling them.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Preserve new-reader parsing of legacy manifests with no pins. Add a pre-feature-verifier fixture…'; AC line: 'A pre-feature reader authenticates the complete stored manifest, then ignores additive pin lines for field interpretation.'
scenarios: Feature is deployed and several tickets acquire pin-bearing manifests. A bug forces a rollback to pre-feature code. The claim gate on the old code encounters manifests containing `plan-material-pin:` lines. If the old verifier is strict about unknown lines (e.g., treats them as malformed steps), those attestations fail verification, blocking all claim gates until the tickets are re-reviewed.

After rollback, `resign_plan_review` reads the sidecar and re-signs — but without the pin-collection logic, the new manifest has no pins; the old reader would accept it. Plan is silent on whether this re-sign path is the intended recovery or whether operators are on their own.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Implementation or Verification section: 'A pre-feature code verifier (the HMAC or DSSE verifier) treats `plan-material-pin:` lines as ordinary manifest entries; the signature check passes because it covers all lines including the new ones, and the pre-feature claim gate ignores unknown lines (it does not enumerate expected prefixes). Therefore rollback leaves signed manifests intact and verifiable.' If that is NOT the existing behavior, document the operator recovery path (e.g., `resign_plan_review` as a recovery tool to produce pin-free manifests under the old code).

findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshotError reason codes are referenced as a 'closed' enum throughout the plan and AC/Verification, but their actual vocabulary is never defined. The Implementation enumerates the triggering conditions in prose (missing/deleted, ambiguous/malformed, reducer-error, canonical-ID-mismatch, ticket-store-read failures), yet never lists the corresponding reason-code strings/symbols that the structured ERROR log must emit and that tests must cover exhaustively.
findings.location: Implementation §4 ('raises internal PlanRelationSnapshotError with a closed reason code'); AC line: 'Each PlanRelationSnapshotError reason produces a structured ERROR log'; Verification: 'Failure-path tests cover every … PlanRelationSnapshotError reason'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `Reason` enum or `Literal` to the `PlanRelationSnapshotError` definition: e.g. `Literal['missing_deleted', 'ambiguous_reference', 'reducer_error', 'canonical_id_mismatch', 'store_read_failure']`, and reference these exact values in the AC and Verification sections.

findings.finding: AC14 only verifies that PlanRelationSnapshot *carries* the ticket-store revision ('PlanRelationSnapshot carries the ticket-store revision used for bounded sign-time retry'), but the Implementation describes active retry/verification behavior at sign time using that revision; no AC covers the retry trigger, the bound on retries, or the failure mode when the revision changes.
findings.location: AC14 vs. Implementation phase story paragraph

findings.finding: The plan specifies that the global-lock HEAD re-verification happens before signature append+commit, but does not specify the error-handling path when the revision has changed (i.e., before != after under the lock). It is unspecified whether this produces a retry, a new PlanRelationSnapshotError, an INDETERMINATE result, or a silent success with potentially stale pins.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'REVISION_CHANGED' reason code to PlanRelationSnapshotError; when the under-lock HEAD check fails, raise it (or retry once and then raise it). The review boundary already handles PlanRelationSnapshotError → unsigned INDETERMINATE + structured ERROR log, so the existing error path can absorb this case with minimal new code.

findings.finding: The plan specifies a two-part concurrency guard — an outer 'equal before/after revision' bracket around lock-free collection, plus an under-lock HEAD verification before signature append — but never defines what happens when either check detects a mismatch. Without a specified remediation (retry the collection, raise PlanRelationSnapshotError with a named reason code, or surface INDETERMINATE to the caller), the system's behavior under concurrent ticket-store mutation is implementation-defined. This leaves the shared ticket_store_revision state without a complete, owned failure path, violating the 'atomically or fail-safe with defined remediation' requirement.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision … The phase story brackets outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions and verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Implementation section: 'If the outer before/after revision bracket detects a change, raise PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=STORE_REVISION_CHANGED, ...) and return INDETERMINATE — no retry. If the under-lock HEAD verification fails, raise the same error; the sign path aborts without writing the manifest.' This closes both mismatch paths with a single named reason code and no partial-pin state.

findings.finding: The Implementation describes a sign-time retry/verification mechanism — 'verifies the same HEAD under the global lock before signature append+commit' after outside-lock collection with equal before/after revisions — but the Verification section lists no test command or fixture covering what happens when the HEAD changes between collection and lock acquisition, leaving the retry/failure path untested.
findings.location: Implementation (phase story paragraph) vs. Verification section

findings.finding: Acceptance Criterion 15 ('Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations') has no corresponding verification step that actually varies project enforcement configuration and asserts pins still appear.
findings.location: AC: 'Pin emission is unconditional across project configurations.' / Verification section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Verification: 'A parametrized test exercises pin collection with project enforcement both enabled and disabled and asserts related_material is non-empty in both cases.'

findings.finding: The concurrency guard uses `ticket_store_revision` defined as 'the clean tickets tracker HEAD SHA read after ensure_fresh,' but the existing signing infrastructure's `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` reads the MAIN code-repo HEAD, not the tickets-tracker HEAD. The plan's 'under the global lock before signature append+commit' verify step appears to rely on a tickets-tracker HEAD check that has no current implementation anchor, and the signing lock (`append_event`) does not currently perform or expose a tickets-HEAD re-check. This creates a hidden ordering/atomicity assumption: the under-lock HEAD verification mechanism the plan describes does not map onto any existing locked path.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5 ('Expose deterministic PlanRelationSnapshot collection plus ticket_store_revision…')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define an explicit delivery-order constraint or activation gate between this story and child 5967 (e.g., this story's signing paths are behind a feature flag or stub that raises until 5967's atomic lock is in place), or document that the stories ship atomically together, so that signing paths are never live without atomic HEAD-capture-to-commit protection.
evidence: 'This story verifies accurate token capture only; it never acquires the sign lock.'
'Child 5967 exclusively owns before/after mismatch retries, under-lock HEAD re-verification, atomic append+commit, structured retry logging, and exhaustion behavior.'

'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, progressive drift refresh, and resign_plan_review—collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes related_material; no new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless.'
ABSENCE: No ordering constraint or activation gate between this story and child 5967 is stated.
findings.finding: The plan adds pin emission to all three signing paths (ordinary sign_plan_review, progressive drift refresh, resign_plan_review) and captures ticket_store_revision via tracker_head_sha(), but explicitly defers sign-lock acquisition and atomic append+commit entirely to child 5967. The plan does not state that 5967 must land before or concurrently with this story. If this story ships independently, those signing paths emit pins and capture a HEAD SHA without atomic protection, creating a TOCTOU window between the tracker_head_sha() read and the eventual manifest commit — a later write could change HEAD between capture and commit with no retry or detection in this story's scope.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'This story verifies accurate token capture only; it never acquires the sign lock. Child 5967 exclusively owns before/after mismatch retries, under-lock HEAD re-verification, atomic append+commit, structured retry logging, and exhaustion behavior.' and 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, progressive drift refresh, and resign_plan_review—collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes related_material'
scenarios: If this story is merged and deployed before child 5967, a concurrent signing operation on the same review could capture a stale or superseded tracker HEAD SHA, emit it as ticket_store_revision in the manifest, and sign it — with no mismatch detection, retry, or atomic protection — producing a permanently incorrect ticket_store_revision in a signed manifest that cannot be corrected without re-signing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a statement in the Implementation section such as: 'The signing paths added by this story MUST NOT be enabled in production until child 5967 lands; gate them behind the existing enforcement flag or a dedicated feature flag so that tracker_head_sha capture and manifest commit are never exercised without the atomic lock and retry logic from 5967.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete rejection behavior of `tracker_head_sha` for dirty/unmerged state: name the exception class raised, the reason string (if any), and whether it maps to an existing `PlanRelationSnapshotError` reason or a new dedicated error type.
evidence: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker after ensure_fresh and rejects dirty or unmerged state'
AC 15: 'tracker_head_sha captures the clean tickets-tracker 40-hex HEAD after ensure_fresh' — verifies capture only, not rejection path
No exception type, reason string, or caller-visible behavior is named for the rejection path
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha 'rejects' dirty or unmerged state without naming the exception type or error message contract, leaving the executor to guess how rejection should surface and making AC 15 partially underspecified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) … rejects dirty or unmerged state'
scenarios: An executor implementing the rejection could raise a generic RuntimeError, a domain-specific TrackerDirtyError, or silently return None — each producing different caller semantics and a different test interface.
AC 15 only tests the happy path; a test for rejection behavior (which exception? what message?) cannot be written without a stated contract.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'On dirty or unmerged state, tracker_head_sha raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason="store-read-failure") (or a named TrackerStateError) so callers can handle it uniformly alongside other collection failures.' Then add an AC or sub-bullet exercising this path.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
